repaired_question_bank.question: A model performs impressively on many tasks after being trained on massive web-scale text. What concern does this raise about its apparent ability?
choices: It may be benefiting from massive exposure rather than robustly generalizing like a human learner.
It must have solved the symbol-grounding problem completely.
It cannot use memorized patterns at all.
It is guaranteed to generalize better than any recurrent model on limited data.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Strong behavior after massive exposure can reflect pattern familiarity as well as real generalization. A good diagnostic asks whether the model can handle genuinely novel tasks or limited-data settings.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why is benchmark contamination a serious evaluation problem for large pretrained models?
choices: The benchmark may no longer be an independent test if its examples appeared in pretraining data.
It makes the benchmark too hard for smaller models only.
It prevents models from producing fluent text.
It means human-written benchmarks cannot contain coding problems.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Evaluation should measure unseen performance. If benchmark items are present in pretraining data, high scores may reflect memorization of test examples rather than the intended capability.

repaired_question_bank.question: What makes low-resource and primarily oral languages especially difficult for current data-hungry NLP methods?
choices: They may lack enough written data to support the same pretraining approach used for high-resource languages.
They are impossible to represent with neural networks in principle.
They never have grammar or regular linguistic patterns.
They only need larger English benchmarks to be solved.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Modern pretraining depends heavily on large text corpora. Languages with limited written material or mainly oral use do not provide the same data foundation, even if they have many speakers or cultural importance.
